votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions and logic, including the recursive list processing and sorting functions.
The Python code is a direct and faithful translation of the Coq functions, preserving the exact logic for collecting and sorting coordinates.

The Python code is a direct, recursive translation of the Coq code. It implements the same coordinate collection logic and the same insertion sort algorithm with the specific ordering (ascending row, descending column) defined in the Coq specification.

The Python code faithfully translates all Coq definitions. The helper functions collect_coords_row, collect_all_coords, insert_coord, and sort_coords follow the same logic as their Coq counterparts. The sorting order (rows ascending, columns descending within same row) is implemented identically. The spec function problem_87_spec checks equality with get_row_impl in both versions. All concrete inputs will produce the same results.

Python mirrors the Coq implementation: it collects coordinates row-wise, then insertion-sorts by row ascending and column descending, and the spec checks equality to this result. Integer comparisons match Z ordering, so outputs align for all inputs.

Python mirrors Coq: it collects all (row,col) for elements equal to x with rows/cols starting at 0, then sorts by row ascending and within each row by column descending using the same insertion sort logic. For all concrete inputs, results match.

Both implementations collect all (row, column) coordinates where elements equal x with 0-based indices, then perform the same insertion sort: rows ascending and, within equal rows, columns descending. The Python functions mirror the Coq logic for collection and sorting, yielding identical results on all inputs.
